summary:
The development of cattle pastoralism in the Saharan region during the period when the [[event:17|Saharan Green Period]] made the desert habitable represents an important chapter in the broader story of [[event:23|African cattle domestication]]. By 7000 to 6000 BCE, communities across the central and western Sahara — in regions now covered by the deserts of Algeria, Niger, Libya, and Chad — were herding cattle across a landscape of grasslands, lakes, and rivers quite unlike the arid wasteland that exists today.

Rock art from the Saharan massifs, particularly from the Tassili n'Ajjer, Fezzan, and Tibesti regions, depicts cattle herding scenes from this era, showing long-horned bovids accompanied by human herders in compositions that indicate these animals were central to the communities' way of life. The Cattle Period of Saharan rock art corresponds broadly with the wetter climatic phase, and the imagery suggests that cattle held not only economic but deep cultural significance — comparable to the role of cattle in later African pastoral societies.

Saharan cattle pastoralists practiced a mobile lifestyle suited to managing herds across seasonally variable grassland environments. As the Sahara began to dry after approximately 5500 BCE, these herding communities migrated southward and eastward, carrying their cattle and their pastoral traditions into sub-Saharan Africa. The descendants of these Saharan pastoralists can be traced genetically and culturally in numerous African populations, and the importance of cattle in many African societies today — as wealth, ritual animals, and food sources — reflects this deep historical connection to the pastoral traditions of the Green Sahara.
